metadata.collection: Kinneret Sites — Galilean Ministry Heritage Properties

sites.name: Mt. of Beatitudes
sites.description: Modern octagonal memorial church (1936–38) on a spur 1 km NE of Tabgha, with hospice, pastoral gardens and panoramic view over the Sea of Galilee and surrounding ministry sites.
sites.significanceSummary: The Sermon on the Mount is a prime symbol of Jesus' ministry, laying the basis for Christian Charity, Piety and Grace. The site preserves spiritual significance through its serene landscape and association with the 'sacred triangle' of Capernaum–Tabgha–Beatitudes.
sites.highlight: Spiritual significance carried entirely by landscape, tradition and setting — no archaeological event-trace.

valueSpecifications.specification: Sermon on the Mount = foundation of Christian Charity, Piety and Grace; Beatitudes replace Mosaic commandments
Serene qualities preserve spiritual significance; panoramic view over ministry area; rural-agricultural setting unchanged
Tradition since early Christianity; Egeria's testimony; papal visits 1964, 2000

sites.integrityNote: Modern church (1930s) is only built element; no ancient structural remains on this specific spur; landscape setting intact
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; inferred from landscape-dependent significance
ownership: Franciscan Missionary Sisters of the Immaculate Heart of Mary
sites.comparativeBasis: Part of 'sacred triangle' with Capernaum and Tabgha
sites.argumentStrengthNote: No archaeological trace of the event; depends entirely on tradition + setting
sites.visitorAccessNote: Open to public; hospice for pilgrims

sites.name: Capernaum
sites.description: Large archaeological site on the N shore of the Sea of Galilee: Franciscan compound with 1st c. CE village remains, House of St. Peter, Byzantine octagonal church and monumental white-limestone synagogue; Greek-Orthodox compound with multi-period remains.
sites.significanceSummary: The most important Holy site on the N shore; Jesus' 'home away from home.' Mentioned 21 times in the Gospels; second only to Jerusalem in narrative intensity. Reveals a fishing village from Jesus' time with continuous sacral activity and unique Jewish-Christian coexistence.
sites.highlight: Strongest evidential basis in the collection — archaeological corroboration + dense Gospel narrative.

valueSpecifications.specification: Jesus' 'home away from home'; 21 Gospel mentions; more than half of Gospel narratives located here
Fishing village from time of Jesus confirmed; House of St. Peter identified under later churches
Jewish and Christian monumental architecture standing side-by-side in Byzantine period
Largest and most magnificent Byzantine synagogue in Galilee — conspicuous white limestone edifice

sites.integrityNote: Extensive archaeological remains; some restored; modern memorial church above ancient remains; active conservation
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; inferred from major pilgrimage site with exposed remains
sites.protectionStatus: Church property (Franciscan + Greek Orthodox); NPA visitor center
ownership: Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land
sites.comparativeBasis: Second only to Jerusalem in Gospel narrative intensity; most important site on N shore of Sea of Galilee
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Identification solid; archaeological + textual evidence converge; most complete argument in collection
sites.visitorAccessNote: Franciscan compound open daily; Greek Orthodox compound partially accessible; NPA dock and visitor center
keyDates: 4th-5th c. CE: Byzantine churches and synagogue

sites.name: Chorazin
sites.subRegion: 4 km N of Capernaum, basaltic spur
sites.description: Archaeological National Park on a basaltic spur 4 km N of Capernaum. Partly restored late-Roman/Byzantine village with monumental basalt synagogue, richly carved elements, and megalithic dolmen tombs.
sites.significanceSummary: Jesus preached and performed miracles here ('Woe to you, Chorazin!'). A rare example of a typical Talmudic village in a pristine landscape virtually unchanged in 4,000 years. Illustrates the spatial frame of Jesus' ministry.
sites.highlight: Dual argument: Gospel association + typological rarity of intact Talmudic village-landscape.

valueSpecifications.specification: Chastisement passage: 'Woe to you, Chorazin!' — Jesus preached, performed miracles, was refused
Rare example of a typical Talmudic late Roman – Byzantine village
Pristine natural and agricultural landscape which has not changed much in the last 4,000 years
Illustrates the spatial frame of the activity of Jesus in the region

sites.integrityNote: Partly restored synagogue and private quarters; landscape unchanged; northern quarter unfenced and unexcavated
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; northern quarter unfenced and vulnerable
sites.comparativeBasis: Rare preserved example of typical Talmudic village; landscape unchanged 4,000 years
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Identification well-established; dual argument (Gospel + typological) is robust
sites.visitorAccessNote: National Park open daily; northern quarter unfenced open-air ruin
keyDates: Late Roman–Byzantine: Village expansion, synagogue built

sites.name: Kafr Kanna
sites.subRegion: 4 km N of Nazareth, Nazareth range
sites.type: Living village with churches and archaeological remains
sites.description: Living village 4 km N of Nazareth with churches in an ancient nucleus, including the Franciscan Wedding Church (1880s) built over archaeological remains of earlier structures; Greek Orthodox church; mosques; agricultural surroundings.
sites.significanceSummary: Long revered as the site of Jesus' first miracle — turning water into wine. Continuous pilgrimage since the Byzantine period. Authenticity of the Cana identification has been questioned but is an ancient tradition.
sites.highlight: Only living-heritage site in the collection; acknowledged doubt about identification is part of its own record.

valueSpecifications.specification: First miracle of Jesus — water into wine; 'the event where Jesus went public'
From the Byzantine period onwards, repeatedly visited by pilgrims
Functioning village; Muslim majority + Christian minority; churches and mosques in ancient nucleus
Text itself states 'its authenticity has been questioned' — the doubt is part of the record

sites.integrityNote: Living village — continuous modification; archaeological remains beneath modern structures; ancient nucleus partially preserved
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; inferred from living village context with expanding population
sites.protectionStatus: Church properties (Franciscan, Greek Orthodox); village under local municipality
ownership: Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land
sites.comparativeBasis: Identification debated; competing site at Khirbet Qana
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Text itself acknowledges identification is questioned; weakest evidentiary basis in collection
sites.visitorAccessNote: Churches open to visitors; village is public; archaeological remains visible in church interiors

sites.name: Magdala
sites.description: Archaeological site along the W lakeshore: 1st c. CE public and private buildings, well-paved Cardo Maximus, harbor installations, 1st c. fishing boat (displayed nearby), Byzantine monastery with mosaic floor, 8th-9th c. large building.
sites.significanceSummary: Hometown of Mary Magdalene. The best-preserved 1st c. CE Roman town-center in Eastern Galilee — complementing scriptural descriptions and deepening understanding of the cultural context of Jesus' activity.
sites.highlight: Best-preserved 1st c. urban fabric in the region — the archaeology speaks independently of tradition.

valueSpecifications.specification: Hometown of Mary Magdalene — 'one of the most revered disciples, consecrated worldwide'
Best-preserved 1st century CE Roman town-center in Eastern Galilee
Remains 'complement the scriptures and provide deeper understanding of the cultural context within which Jesus was acting'

sites.integrityNote: Partly excavated; significant unexcavated areas; 1st c. fabric well-preserved; ongoing conservation
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; private land + government land creates fragmented protection
sites.protectionStatus: Mixed: Franciscan Custodia (archaeological site), Government land, Private property
ownership: Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land
sites.comparativeBasis: Best-preserved 1st c. CE Roman town-center in Eastern Galilee
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Identification solid; 'best-preserved' claim needs formal comparative basis but evidence is strong
sites.visitorAccessNote: Partially open; active excavation areas restricted; Franciscan site accessible

sites.name: Mt. Tabor
sites.description: Uniquely rounded mountain (~1,800 ft) in the Jezreel Valley with two summit compounds: Franciscan Church of the Transfiguration + monastery; Greek Orthodox church + monastery. Archaeological remains of multiple periods.
sites.significanceSummary: Traditional site of the Transfiguration — Jesus revealed as founder and leader of the community, renewing hope of the Parousia. Tradition probably dates from earliest Christianity (4th c. remains). Natural beauty and splendor.
sites.highlight: Geographic outlier; competing identification with Mt. Hermon not addressed in the text.

valueSpecifications.specification: Transfiguration = Jesus revealed as divine; 'renewed in Christians the hope of the Parousia, the Second Coming'
Uniquely rounded mountain; natural beauty; likely hosted pagan worship before Christianity
Multiple church phases from 4th c.; Franciscan + Greek Orthodox compounds; continuous sacred use

sites.integrityNote: Modern churches over ancient remains; continuous sacred use; archaeological remains from multiple periods; natural setting intact
sites.threatNote: Not stated; competing tradition (Mt. Hermon) not addressed
ownership: Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Ancient tradition but not unique to this mountain; some traditions point to Mt. Hermon; text doesn't address competing identification
sites.visitorAccessNote: Both compounds open to visitors; access road to summit

sites.name: Tabgha
sites.type: Complex of religious compounds and archaeological sites
sites.period: Roman quarry; 4th c. CE – Crusader – modern
sites.description: Multiple compounds on the NW shore: Benedictine Church of the Multiplication (modern reconstruction over 5th c. basilica with famous mosaic floors); Franciscan Church of the Primacy of St. Peter; ANSMI compound with main spring; Byzantine Beatitude chapel on northern slope; lush valley with ancient anchors.
sites.significanceSummary: Since the 4th c. CE, identified with three key Gospel events: multiplication of loaves and fish, Sermon on the Mount, and post-resurrection appearance. Contains magnificent 5th c. mosaic floors. Continuous pilgrimage; a layered palimpsest of sacred commemoration.
sites.highlight: Richest layered site: three Gospel events, five church phases, outstanding mosaics, and Egeria's eyewitness account.

valueSpecifications.specification: Three Gospel events at one site: multiplication of loaves, Sermon on the Mount, post-resurrection appearance
5th c. mosaic floors — 'certainly the most admirable feature'; basket with loaves and fish; Nile-landscape transepts; 'unusually imaginative and of high quality'
Five chapel phases at Church of Primacy; palimpsest of construction from 4th–13th c.
Egeria's 4th c. detailed testimony anchors traditions to specific physical locations
Superb winter fishing ground; springs exploited for agriculture; 'rural scene certainly reminiscent of the ancient landscape'

sites.integrityNote: Modern reconstruction incorporating ancient remains; well-preserved mosaics; continuous sacred use; Byzantine chapel on slope hermetically protected
sites.threatNote: Not stated in source; inferred from multiple owners and high visitor volume
sites.protectionStatus: Church properties (Benedictine, Franciscan, ANSMI); no unified management
ownership: Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land
sites.comparativeBasis: Mosaics among finest in early Christian Palestine; harbor among best-preserved ancient anchorages on Sea of Galilee
sites.argumentStrengthNote: Multiple independent evidence lines: Egeria testimony, five church phases, outstanding mosaics, archaeological stratigraphy
sites.visitorAccessNote: Church of Multiplication open to public; Church of Primacy open; ANSMI compound not public; Byzantine chapel closed behind fence
keyDates: 4th c. CE: First churches (Multiplication + Primacy + Beatitude chapel)
5th c. CE: Monumental basilica with mosaic floors
1980: New Church of Multiplication built over remains
